overview: Configuration-management tools (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) solve 'configuration drift' by applying and enforcing a standard configuration across many devices, using REST/JSON/YAML as the underlying data/interchange formats.
definitions: Configuration Drift: when a device's actual configuration deviates from its intended baseline over time.
Idempotency: applying the same configuration repeatedly produces the same end state without errors or duplication.
REST API: a stateless client/server architecture using HTTP verbs to perform CRUD operations.
CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete — mapped to POST, GET, PATCH/PUT, DELETE.
core_concepts: Ansible: Push model, agentless (uses SSH), playbooks written in YAML, uses Jinja2 templating.
Puppet: Pull model, agent-based, configuration written as Manifests in a Ruby DSL.
Chef: Pull model, agent-based, configuration written as Recipes/Cookbooks in a Ruby DSL.
JSON syntax: objects use { }, arrays use [ ], and each entry is a Key:Value pair.
protocol_operation: Ansible connects to each target device via SSH (push) and applies the playbook's tasks in order.
Puppet/Chef agents periodically pull their intended configuration from a central server and reconcile local state to match.
packet_flow: Ansible control node -> SSH connection to target device -> playbook tasks executed in order -> idempotency check (if the correct config already exists, no change is made, no error is thrown).

config_commands.purpose: Run an Ansible playbook against the devices defined in its inventory (control-node CLI, not IOS).

cli_examples.title: REST CRUD-to-HTTP-verb mapping
cli_examples.code:
POST   -> Create
GET    -> Read
PATCH/PUT -> Update
DELETE -> Delete

troubleshooting.symptom: An Ansible playbook to change interface speed on 50 routers fails to connect to all of them.
troubleshooting.cause: SSH is not enabled/reachable on the target routers, which is the transport Ansible normally uses to reach network devices.
troubleshooting.fix: Verify SSH is enabled on the routers (crypto keys generated, VTY transport set to ssh) and reachable from the Ansible control node.

troubleshooting.symptom: Ansible finds a router that already has the correct configuration.
troubleshooting.cause: This is expected idempotent behavior, not a bug.
troubleshooting.fix: Ansible will not rewrite the configuration if the desired state already matches — this property is called idempotency.

best_practices: Use templates and variables so devices with similar roles share a consistent configuration baseline.
Watch YAML indentation carefully — incorrect indentation is the most common Ansible parsing error.
interview_questions: Why is Ansible described as agentless while Puppet and Chef are agent-based?
Explain 'idempotency' and why it matters for automation tools.
What are the four HTTP verbs used for CRUD operations, and what does each map to?
exam_traps: Mixing up Push (Ansible) vs. Pull (Puppet/Chef) models.
Incorrect YAML indentation causing playbook parse failures.
enterprise_notes: Config-management tooling is central to Cisco SDA/SDN operational models, letting fabric-wide changes be applied consistently instead of device-by-device.
